informal (talking about music, sports, hobbies, going out with friends, social media,) the cause of poor academic performance. Presentation

children should be allowed to take decisions on matters and concerning them. and

Make inferences (about purpose, intention, theme/ message) and simple connections to real life and personal experiences.

content_standards.statement: Apply the knowledge of phrases and clauses and their functions in Communication.

indicators.statement: Use noun phrases accurately in context knowledge of phrases
exemplars: Use noun phrases in texts. and clauses and and their functions in e.g. Communication. i. I want a dress for Christmas. and
The brown kente vase is up for sale. and
I saw a beautiful bird.
Kwaku lives in a blue dorm. Presentation
Having been a chef, he knew how to bake).

Determine and discuss the functions of noun phrases in texts. Functions: o subject of sentences – The tall lady teaches English in Bakano JHS. o subject complement – The man is an awesome welder. o direct object of transitive verbs – I like the sleek Apple laptop. o objects of prepositions – He bought a vehicle for the winner of the race. o apposition – My hometown, Tamale, is a wonderful place. Kwame Aidoo, the class prefect of Grade 8, is my friend. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

indicators.statement: Demonstrate command using multiple adjectives in the correct order, and using quantifiers effectively in speaking and writing and
exemplars: Use adjective phrases to provide vivid descriptions of: and o Participants - The dazzlingly beautiful winner walked down and the aisle. The man covered with mud crawled his way out of the mudslide. o Settings – The journey begun on a rainy Monday morning. Presentation o Materials - Amma is wearing a bright coloured African print. o Objects - The old rickety bus stopped in the middle of the road.

indicators.statement: Use more complex phrasal verbs accurately in speech and writing
exemplars: Identify phrasal verbs in texts and determine what they mean in and context (come after, come along, blow up, make up, etc). and
Use phrasal verbs in developing meaningful paragraphs. and Presentation © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

indicators.statement: Use the knowledge of the adverbial phrase and its functions
exemplars: Use adverb phrases to: and o Describe how — e.g. Aysha passed the exam quite easily. and o Describe where — e.g. I met Kwame near the bridge. and o Describe why — e.g. The law was abolished to end discrimination of girls in school. Presentation o Describe when — e.g. The disgraced thief left the scene as quickly as possible.

content_standards.statement: Demonstrate understanding of the use of conditional tenses in communication

indicators.statement: Use conditional sentences in communication to understanding of the use indicate an impossible condition in the past and its probable of conditional tenses in result and communication
exemplars: Use if- clauses to construct conditional sentences (type 3) to refer and to an impossible condition in the past and its probable result. and
If I had worked harder, I would have passed the exam. You could have been on time if you had caught the bus.
Construct and analyse given conditional sentences (1-3) into main Presentation clauses and subordinate clauses (if-clauses).

indicators.statement: Use defining and non-defining relative clauses appropriately in speech and writing.
exemplars: Apply the functions of dependent and independent clauses in and sentences and texts. and and Presentation © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

exemplars: Use reported speech in oral communication. and

content_standards.statement: Demonstrate mastery of capitalisation and punctuation in communication

indicators.statement: Identify and use punctuation marks (dash, hyphen, mastery of capitalisation bracket) in context and punctuation in
exemplars: Use the dash to: and communication o mark the beginning and the end of an interruption in a sentence. and
My son - where has he gone - will be happy to see you. and o introduce an explanation of a word or an expression earlier mentioned.
It was not a lion - it was a tiger - furiously lashing its way Presentation through the undergrowth. o introduce a list. E.g. Everyone needs good qualities – loyalty, discipline, honesty etc.

Use the hyphen to: o form compound words. E.g. father-in-law o join words in attributive compound. E.g. a well-known artist o join a prefix to a root word. E.g. co-ordinate o mark expressions that look the same but different. E.g. a poor-rate collection; a poor rate-collection. o separate two similar consonant or vowel sounds. E.g. pre-empt, Ross-shire etc. o indicate fractions or multipliers. E.g. two-thirds, hundred-folds etc © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

Use the hyphen at the end of a line when writing to indicate that the word is not hyphenated. It is broken for lack of space. Such words should be broken at a syllable. E.g. education, information etc. o She gave me information about the lost boy. She gave me information about the lost boy.

Use the bracket to: o enclose something that is added to the sentence but not considered to be of major importance. o offer explanation to something that was said earlier. o enclose cross reference. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021

content_standards.statement: Demonstrate appropriate use of vocabulary and spelling conventions in communication

indicators.statement: Interpret vocabulary appropriately in more complex appropriate use texts of vocabulary and
exemplars: Demonstrate appropriate use of vocabulary in context. and spelling conventions in communication and and Presentation © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021

content_standards.statement: Develop, organise and express ideas coherently and cohesively in writing

indicators.statement: Compose logically connected paragraphs to show organise and express unity, completeness and coherence using appropriate cohesive ideas coherently and devices, e.g., connectors, pronouns, repetition of vocabulary or and cohesively in writing grammatical structures and
exemplars: Use coordinating conjunctions to join clauses in compound sentences: and, but, nor, or, so, then, yet. and

Use subordinating conjunctions to join clauses in complex sentences: o time clauses: when, before, after, since, while, as, until Presentation o conditional clauses: if, unless o purpose clauses: in order to, so that o reason clauses: because, since, as o result clauses: so that o concessive clauses: although, though, while o place clauses: where, wherever o clauses of manner: as, like, the way.

Expand sentences in to paragraphs using defining and non-defining relative clauses. E.g. the men who stole the car were jailed. (defining) The women, who stole the car, were jailed. (non-defining) Differences between the two may show in the comma marks.
Use noun phrases, adjectival phrases and adverbial phrases to expand sentences. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

indicators.statement: Develop a paragraph to show paragraph unity and completeness using supporting details (e.g. explanation, elaboration, definition, examples) and
exemplars: Write paragraphs showing the topic sentence, minor and major and supporting sentences using transitional devices to bring about cohesion and coherence, e.g. and o and, again, and then, besides, equally important, finally, o whereas, but, yet, on the other hand, however, nevertheless Presentation o yet, still, however, nevertheless, in spite of, © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

content_standards.statement: Create different paragraphs a given topic (Please check for an omission here)

indicators.statement: Compose more complex paragraphs using different paragraphs a appropriate strategies given topic (Please
exemplars: Write mixed paragraph (paragraph with the topic sentence located and check for an omission in the middle) and periodic paragraphs (paragraph with the topic here) and sentence at the end) using transitional devices to create effect and purpose. and
Organise sentences in a logical sequence to create coherence and beauty that sustains interest in an introductory paragraph. Presentation

Use logical connectors to link sentences in a paragraph: o ordering ideas: firstly, secondly, finally, etc. o addition: moreover, furthermore, in addition, etc. o similarity: similarly, likewise, in the same way, etc. o contrast: however, nevertheless, although, though, on the other hand, etc. o cause/effect: because, therefore, as a result, consequently, etc. o conditions: if, provided that, unless, etc. o sequence in time: next, soon, after, then, later, suddenly, afterwards, etc.
Use logical connectors, pronouns and the repetition of words, synonyms and antonyms to create cohesion and coherence and unity and completeness of paragraph. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021

exemplars: Compose an argumentative piece suitable following appropriate and format/style using process approach. and

Write a rejoinder for publication noting the following: o An address to the rejoinder. (own address .... ) o A date. After the address comes a date o A reference should be included. All rejoinders require references. o The recipient’s address. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

show what needs to be done Presentation
indicate how it needs to be done
tell why it will work
Use appropriate, objective language to explain points.
Organise ideas and points in an information text logically.

Using selected topics, write reports that are devoid of decisions based on personal bias, cultural differences and any other criterion that cannot be measured or proven © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

Use formal language, avoid using short forms and abbreviations.
Edit/Proofread the writing for sense or meaning, and effect.
Publish writing using various media including ICT. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

indicators.statement: Compose short text (flyers, posters, invitation cards, email, etc. ) for different purposes and audiences
exemplars: Write flyers or posters of varied kinds using: and o Small size e.g. A5 paper and o Eye-catching headline, slogan or logo and o Brief description of product o List of benefits or offers Presentation o Contact information e.g. website o Include a visual and or a design element

Design an invitation card to incorporate the following: o Nature of the event o Where it will take place o Date and time (could include dress code) o Name of invitee (could include RSVP) o Could have a visual and or a design element

Write emails to contacts, parents or teachers ensuring that the following are present: o The recipient’s address–which, in most cases, is the recipient’s name and the server point. For example, aziz65(name)@gmail. (server). o : These may be additional recipients whose attention are called to the email. o Subject: This is a summary of the content of the email. o Message o Sender’s name and contact. © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021 SUBJECT SPECIFIC PRACTICES

indicators.statement: Write articles (short reports, letters and case studies) on given issues for publication

indicators.statement: Compose speeches for different purposes and occasions.
exemplars: Compose a speech: and o Tell what you are going to tell them (introduction) and o Tell them (body). o Tell them what you told them (conclusion). and

Use the following to create effect: o Emotive adjectives e.g. ‘Wonderful, magnificent, fantastic, disgraceful, shocking, outrageous.’ o Tripling e.g. “It’s your country, it’s my country, it’s our future!” o Repetition o Parallel sentence structure Presentation o Powerful imagery e.g. “I have a dream.” o Use of ‘we’ to include everyone (inclusive language) e.g. “We all know… you and I agree…” © NaC CA, Ministry of Education 2021

content_standards.statement: Use literary devices (imagery) in texts • Use imagery such as personification, simile, metaphors, idiomatic expressions in speech and writing. • Create and apply imagery such as personification, simile, metaphors, idiomatic expressions.
